speaker: interviewer
text: Marika Somerstein. Today is December 19, 1995. We were speaking before about the sequence of events of how your father attempted to save the family. He had made arrangements with the local monastery to hide himself and your mother, and be hidden with a private family. You recall the grandmother. You recall the family  over there. You actually succeeded in making it to the-- to the house. The grandmother was nice to you, but the family not so much. You prefer not to go into that. Your-- your parents-- did they ever make it to the monastery?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they never did. They were taken to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the arrangements were made in March, and the ghetto was dissolved towards the middle of June. And they were taken away at the end of May. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned during the break, the bombings that you had experienced. Can you tell us about those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from the time that this family took me in till the Russians liberated Nyiregyhaza, there were-- almost every night there were bombings. And because of the fact that I was hiding and they were afraid to be caught, every night when the bomb airplanes came, they went to a shelter and I was left in a bed under covers alone. And that-- that is the most horrible part of the-- my life that I can remember, being alone in the dark and the bombings night after night after night and I was all alone. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How much time did you spend hidden in that home?

speaker: interviewee
text: A year, a little over a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would have been from the end of May of 1944 until--

speaker: interviewee
text: And liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was-- that was the home that you were taken to when your aunts took you off the pile of baggages, walked you to the temple. You took off the star. You walked-- you walked through town, met a gentleman in a movie theater, and that was the home that he took you to.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not directly, but after a while, first he-- it's complicated. But eventually, we ended up in his home where he was hiding. The man and the woman didn't agree on having me there. So it wasn't a pleasant-- it was a very unpleasant situation. They made me work very hard. They treated me like I was a servant and the children were very unkind to me. So when my uncle came home, it was a wonderful surprise.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: But he died after three weeks being home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what your time was like in that-- in that home where you were being hidden. If anyone would see you, they would immediately recognize you. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they would. Unfortunately, when I was a little girl, my-- my mother sent in a picture to a-- a coffee commercial and we won the prize. And there where big signs all over Hungary of drink coffee and my face was on it as a child. And everyone knew it was a-- a Jewish child. So I had a familiar face, which didn't make life easy for these people that were hiding me. Because it was just an immediate-- immediately, they would know who I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your days like during that year? What-- what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: They hid you in that special bedroom under the covers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to go outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you read books? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't allowed to do anything. I laid in bed. Once in a while, at night, they let me go out into the courtyard late. Once in a while, the grandmother let me help her cook. But basically, I don't remember. I was just-- I did nothing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you mentioned that last moment that you saw your parents when your aunts took you off of the baggage, did you exchange any words at that time with your parents? Did you speak to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, nothing at all. I-- I cried for my mother for a long time. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't remember anything about that at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: About that leaving. You mentioned that there was one incident where your father had joined you in a drink of liqueur, and he told you that he was leaving and that you would have to take care of Mom. When did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That happened in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was trying to tell you, my-- I remember him as a very elegant man. He did things differently than most people I ever met. And he took a little crystal glass with the liquor and toasted. And he had said, I will not come back, but I want you to carry on and do things nicely, make things beautiful. And after that, we never spoke about it again. He told me that my mother doesn't want me to leave and I-- I-- I-- I-- if I remember now, it would be he defied my mother by sending me away. Consequently, they both went to Auschwitz. My mother did survive because of the sisters that had been with her, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who together they kind of took care of each other. My father was killed immediately, very, very, very soon. I think it-- he was taken in end of May. Beginning of June, they got to Auschwitz and by August, he was dead. His stomach, he had a very bad ulcer. He was taken to Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, I visited Mauthausen this year. And I saw for the first-- have you been there? Horrendous place, horrendous. I-- I never knew that, how bad it was until I just-- just saw it. That's where he was killed. How did you find out what happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: A friend came back to Hungary and gave me dates, gave me circumstances of how he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had been in camp with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The friend's name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother went to Auschwitz, too, but because of the cooperative effort of her and her sisters, they were able to somehow support each other and survive. Did your mother ever relate to you what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: In camp? Yes, as a matter of fact, quite a bit. They-- my mother was the biggest of them and the oldest. She-- she was-- she almost didn't make it, but as I said before, they hurt them. They were beaten, they were-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they-- they did not know what was going on. They really did not know about the gas chambers around them. They-- they did know, however, that had I been with my mother, they would have never been able to be together. I mean, my mother did survive because I wasn't there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My other aunt, who also had a child my age, the child never came back. When my mother and my aunts came back from the camp, I remember them coming into town. There were all wearing red blouses because they had red flags. The Russians liberated them and from the red flags they were-- they made this. So the four ladies arrived in the red.

speaker: interviewee
text: Made from Communist flags.

speaker: interviewee
text: Made some Communist flags. And gray flannel slacks made from blankets. And they-- they said-- they told some bad stories about what happened. My mother didn't know too much, because she-- she was really in a working camp. My-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mother didn't know that my father passed away until-- until we-- until she came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she found out afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother and all of her sisters survived that way. And then you mentioned the uncle, who returned and who actually found you in the home of the individual who was hiding you. Tell us about that. Well, he-- he was young. He was a very handsome young man.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immy. And he took me back home to my house where we used to live with my parents. And the Gestapo headquarters used to be there. So everything was destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gestapo headquarters was your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was in my house, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So originally, your house was one home that belonged to you. Then it became part of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were at least 17 families in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the ghetto was liquidated, then the Gestapo made the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Headquarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: House their Gestapo headquarters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. So when I got back there with my uncle, there was nothing left. And everything was destroyed, everything was taken. And he hired someone to take care of me, and went to Budapest to find my parents. He had no idea where they are. And while he was looking for them among the dead and so on, he got typhus. He-- he contracted, and he died. So once again, I was left alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the end of the war? You remember the bombing, clearly, the Allied bombings. Do you remember when the war ended? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The people that were hiding me were-- came in one morning and said that the Russians are coming, and that maybe my parents are coming with them. I mean, the naivete of the people were amazing. And there were big street fights. People were hungry, had no money, no food. They were eating the horses. The horses were killed in the town, and that's what we ate. That's the only food that was around for about three weeks. There was no food to be had.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is while the Russians are attacking in an attempt to capture the city, and the Germans were trying to defend it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have one very fond memory of a Russian soldier, when he came and came into the house and wanted to-- they took, they looted quite a bit, the Russian soldiers. But this one man started to take some of the things from this family, and they said, well, this is a Jewish child. So he sat down and he left them everything. And he kept coming back and bringing candy and food to the family. And about a week after liberation, he invited all his friends, like 20 Russian soldiers. We had a big banquet of food, and I was seated at the head of the table as the young Jewish child. And they danced with me, and music. It was-- it was amazing.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was no communication. I didn't speak Russian, obviously, but they understood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a Jewish soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I wonder. He had to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Josef.

speaker: interviewer
text: Josef. His last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once he discovered that you were Jewish, he brought food for the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And afterwards, established a banquet to celebrate the fact that you had survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, what a-- what a insight this man had, thinking back. And young, he was very young. I remember he was very young, a very jolly and happy man.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the family walked into the room and told you the war was over, what-- what was your understanding of that? What was your feeling about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That my parents are coming home. But that-- it took a long, long time till my parents finally-- or my mother finally got home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till your mother came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Months and months and months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during those months and months, between the time that you knew the war had ended and they actually came into town wearing the red shirts and the gray flannel slacks, what-- what-- what did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I stayed with a neighbor. I told you my uncle died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left that house.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got-- I stayed in-- I was in my house, and the neighbor took me. Then an aunt came from-- my father's sister came, who is now living in Israel. Her is [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewer
text: And her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schonfeld. But right now, I mean, it's-- her name is different now, but that was my father's sister, younger sister. They were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her married name now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now? Yes. Oh my god. Neaman. N-E-A-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she lives in Israel today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel. And her husband is-- I think was born in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Doctor Neaman. Emil Neaman. She's my favorite aunt. Oh, I shouldn't say that on tape. She's lovely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she took care of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: She took care of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until the time when your mom and your aunts came back to the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nine years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the-- the-- the most interesting part of the liberation to me is I was the only child left in Nyiregyhaza. Out of all the Jewish children, not one came back, except me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not one remained in the town--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And not one returned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody returned. No one.

speaker: interviewer
text: As far as you know, you're the only child that remained.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's not as far as I know. It's a fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a fact.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because every mother and father that came back after the war used to come to our home, after my mother and sisters and all came back, just to touch me, just to-- I was the most popular kid in town. No. There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: The only kid.

speaker: interviewee
text: No child.

speaker: interviewer
text: No child survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were all deported to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned that you returned to the town after the war to take a look at it and see it. Can you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my husband, Stuart, who I met in 1975, was very interested in my story. And I realized that, up until then, I never spoke about it. And when-- he really wanted me to go back with him and show him where I was born. So we went back. We went to Budapest, and that was a nice surprise. Budapest is a beautiful city. And then we drove to Nyiregyhaza. And I remembered, I had a picture of the streets, my home. I was very proud of this house, because I remembered it to be a palace. It's not a palace, but I remembered it being one. And we drove into town, and it was so much bigger. They built a lot of different streets. The streets were paved. Some of the streets I remember being dirt roads. And I remembered when my grandmother's house was in grandmother's store. I remembered everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was still there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was still there. And we went to my-- my street, and I went to my-- my home. And I-- three families, three communist families were living in this house right now. And so we looked around. And all of a sudden, from nowhere, I started to tell my husband that, you see, this is where the German soldiers came into the house and asked me to leave in the ghetto when they were taking us away. I wanted a glass of water, and I went to get it. And he said [SPEAKING GERMAN] And nobody ever told me to get out in my life. I was a spoiled little girl. And the harshness, I'll never forget that. And I started to explain this to my husband, that here I was in this room, and then the Germans soldiers were in this room. And all of a sudden, all I could see is German soldiers. So Stuart--

speaker: interviewer
text: You actually saw the German soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw them. I saw them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Although it was 1975.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the most amazing thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were sure they were.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so Stuart got nervous, and he took me out of my home, thinking that once I'm out of the house, it will pass. So we got back in the car, and we started to drive around in town. And then I saw the soldiers. I saw-- and the worst part of it is I saw all the Hungarian Sandor. Sandor was a policeman on a horse with a big rooster's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] feather in his hat. And I saw them. I saw them on every corner, and I saw them-- they-- [SOBBING] [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I saw them. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] I don't know what they did to me, these guys, but every time I think of them-- that's-- this part is tough. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to leave the town because that memory was so vivid.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't remember if, during the time that you were there-- these Sandors, they were Nazis, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Hungarian Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hungarian Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was easy for them just to get off their horse and beating on people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Without cause.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember that happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being Jewish is a good cause.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were stationed in the ghetto, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were everywhere. They were everywhere. You could see them everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were subject-- you and the other Jews were subject to beatings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To violations of your civil rights without cause, without any excuse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Civil rights? I don't think those were-- existed among the Jews in Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My father was beaten before he was killed for stealing an apple in concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I don't eat apples. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And after 1975, after your trip, you left the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we left the town and went back to Budapest, and everything was fine the next day. It was-- it was a very strange experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you reach America after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you reach America after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother remarried, and my stepfather and I were brought here by my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Uncle Micky.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uncle Micky, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had been financed by the family in 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: To emigrate.

speaker: interviewee
text: He brought us out. He brought my aunts, my-- myself, my mother, and stepfather. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came to America in 1951, November 30th. And just about this Christmas, I already was working. You know Jay Thorpe?

speaker: interviewer
text: Jay Corp, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jay Thorpe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jay Thorpe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Department stores. You wouldn't remember. You're too young. It was on 57th Street, and I didn't speak a word of English. And I started to work as a junior model. On the floor, I was modeling, because you didn't need to speak English.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were a model. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then 1955, I got married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1956, I had my first child. His name is Kenneth, who is now a doctor. 1958, I had my daughter, Cherie, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who's married and has two children. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: As a result of the experience that you went through and all that you suffered in that time, is there any message that you would like to-- to tell to the world or to anyone specifically, concerning what happened to you, and possibly what you think we could learn? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Well, I think that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in America, the belief that it can't happen is a very scary notion. I think being married to an American and having American children, I understand how they don't understand that it can happen again. And it's very important that we do what we do just now. I don't enjoy doing what I'm doing, telling you my life. It's very private, and it hurts me to tell you, because I don't want anyone to know. But I'm doing it because I think this is important. And if everyone will pitch in and do what I'm doing, and-- and-- and my children will do it and your children will do it, maybe-- maybe they won't hate us so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. I think that Mr. Somerstein is here, too. He would like to-- to join us. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my mother, Lily Bohm, before she was taken to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that picture was taken in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That is my father, Laszlo Schonfeld, about five years before the war. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That is I at the age of three, I think. What-- was that the year that they had that special competition for the coffee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poster.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was one of the pictures that survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: That were submitted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mother and I on vacation in a seaside town, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- I-- it looks like I'm two years old there, maybe less.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's that with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a friend. I don't know who he is. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That is Uncle Bela Bohm, who survived the war but died later in a drowning accident. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Those are my two cousins that are now living in Israel and I in the Czechoslovakian Tatra. It's right after the war. I'm waiting for my mother to come back from Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is I in Munchen, probably in 1950, thinking I'm very beautiful, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's one picture missing on the upper left-hand. What happened to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have given it to a boyfriend. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is the that brought my stepfather, my mother, and I to America. It wasn't a luxury cruiser.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which port did you come into?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came into New York. We left Bremerhaven and came into New York. It was a horrendous trip. It was in November.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Statue of Liberty, when you first saw it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unbelievable. That is an incredible-- you can't imagine that feeling. That-- that's-- that's unbelievable. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is Kenneth and Cherie at Ken's bar mitzvah. And that's my Lhasa Apso, Tutu. Tutu is no longer with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born in '58, and Kenneth was born in '56. Don't ask me how old they are, because I never remember their ages. I choose not to. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Those are my grandchildren, Jade and Bo. It's a recent picture, about a year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do they live now?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live in Florida, and they're wonderful children. I love them very much. Bo--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bo is 11, and Jade is 4. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That is Stuart my husband, and I think that's me.
